author: Sofia Martínez
title: Solar Installation Digital Marketing: From Inquiry to Signed Contract
description: How solar installers generate qualified leads through local SEO, Google Ads, and content that educates homeowners on ROI and incentives.

sections.p: Solar installation is a high-ticket local service. A homeowner doesn't Google 'solar' on impulse—they're researching a $15,000 to $30,000 investment. Our approach at NetWebMedia focuses on capturing people in the research phase, before they call competitors. We've worked with installers who went from 2-3 qualified leads per month to 8-12, simply by fixing how they show up online and what information they're actually providing.

sections.h2: Google Business Profile Optimization for Solar (This Actually Converts)

sections.p: Your Google Business Profile is your storefront. For solar companies, we optimize for these high-intent searches: 'solar installation [city]', 'solar panels cost [city]', 'solar rebates near me', 'solar installation reviews [city]'. The profile needs to include service areas (list 15-20 nearby towns, not just your main city), high-quality before-and-after images of actual installations, and FAQ posts answering the questions that kill deals early.

Here's what converts: add monthly posts answering 'How much do solar panels cost in [city]?' or 'What federal tax credit can I get?' Your Google Business Profile isn't just for local map visibility—it's where half your web traffic comes from before they ever visit your site. We've seen solar installers boost inquiries 34% just by adding 8 solid FAQ posts over 2 months.

list: ROI calculator blog post (homeowners want to know payback period in their specific state)
Federal tax credit guide updated for current year (solar changes annually)
State-specific incentives breakdown (California, Florida, Texas differ wildly)
How long installation takes + what to expect during process
Roof inspection costs and why some installers charge, others don't
Financing options comparison (cash vs. loan vs. lease vs. PPA)

sections.p: Most solar installers publish zero educational content. Your competitors aren't blogging. That's your advantage. We create 4-6 pillar posts per quarter that directly answer the questions homeowners ask before calling. One installer we worked with published a post titled 'Solar Panel Cost Per Watt in Tennessee 2026' in January. By March, it ranked #2 locally and brought 18 qualified inbound calls—no paid ads needed.

sections.h2: Google Ads Strategy for High-Intent Keywords

sections.p: Solar is competitive on paid search. We run campaigns targeting bottom-of-funnel keywords only: 'solar installation quote [city]', 'best solar company [city]', 'solar panels installed [city]'. Avoid broad keywords like 'solar energy'—you'll hemorrhage budget on people just learning about solar, not ready to buy. Our typical solar clients spend $800-$1,500 per month on Google Ads and average a cost-per-lead of $35-$60 (varies by market competitiveness). One 15% conversion rate from lead to signed contract means 3-4 contracts per month from paid search alone.

Landing page matters as much as ad copy. Generic solar landing pages don't work. Create location-specific landing pages: 'Solar Installation in [City]' with local testimonials, before-and-after photos of actual jobs in that area, and a simple form (name, email, roof type, zip code). Forms with 3-4 fields convert 2.5x better than forms asking 10 questions.

Solar companies live or die by reviews. After a successful installation, send a text-based review request within 48 hours—not an email. Include a direct link to your Google Business Profile review page. Solar installers with 4.6+ star ratings see 22% more inquiry clicks on their Google Business Profile compared to those with 3.8 stars. We've built automated workflows that text customers post-installation with a simple message: 'Thanks for going solar with us! Would you take 60 seconds to leave a review? [link]' This generates 40-50% response rates.

sections.quote: Most solar installers think their job ends when the panels are installed. We treat it as the beginning of the marketing relationship. An unhappy customer becomes a negative review. A delighted customer becomes a referral source and review generator.
